retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Evaluation -- Acute Complications in Patients with SCA
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic crises: It is usually precipitated by parvovirus B-19 and is defined as a rapid drop in Hb at least 3 to 6 gm/dL below the baseline. Patients present with severe fatigue, anemia, shortness of breath, and even syncope. Blood counts show severely low hemoglobin with near-absent reticulocytes. Bone marrow biopsy shows arrest in the pro-normoblast stage in patients with acute parvovirus infections. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia (Nursing) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is the most severe form of sickle cell disease and is the homozygous state for hemoglobin S. Sickle cell anemia is prevalent in Africa, the Middle East, and parts of India. It is common in geographical areas where malaria is widespread. Hemoglobin in most individuals is present in soluble form. However, in sickle cell disease, hemoglobin precipitates as insoluble crystals, leading to an abnormal shape and size of RBCs and subsequent phagocytosis of the affected corpuscles. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Age (per 10-year increase in age) Tricuspid regurgitant jet velocity 2.5 m/s or more Reticulocyte count Log(N-terminal-pro-brain natriuretic peptide) Fetal hemoglobin [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The epidemiological data on SCD is scarce. It is well known that SCD and HbAS are more prevalent in sub-Saharan Africa, where the carrier of HbAS is afforded natural protection against severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria. It is estimated that ~230,000 children were born with SCA, and more than 3.5 million neonates were born with HbAS in sub-Saharan Africa in 2010. an estimated 75% of SCD-related births take place in sub-Saharan Africa. West Africa is home to the largest population of individuals with HbSC disease. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: When a red blood cell is unable to change shape as it passes through the spleen, it will become sequestered and phagocytosis will occur. This is seen in hemoglobinopathies such as sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral blood smear and urine analysis are essential for distinguishing between intravascular and extravascular hemolysis in normocytic anemia. The presence of schistocytes and hemoglobin in the urine suggests microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. However, despite suspected hemolysis, the absence of schistocytes in the peripheral blood smear and the absence of hemoglobin in the urine warrant further investigation. Detection of spherocytes in the peripheral blood smear prompts a direct Coombs test to differentiate autoimmune hemolytic anemia from splenic sequestration in hereditary spherocytosis, aiding diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with SCA, peripheral blood smear shows elongated RBC with tapering ends that look like a sickle (also called drepanocytes). Additional findings are present in a few patients. Howell-Jolly bodies- Remnants of DNA are seen in the RBC and commonly seen in patients in whom the spleen has been removed. Therefore, patients with SCA have auto-splenectomy. Target cells (Leptocytes)- Most commonly seen in patients with Thalassemia. They are seen frequently in sickle-thalassemia syndromes and are sometimes noted in patients with SCA. Polychromatic cells - these are reticulocytes that signify marrow response to hemolysis. Nucleated red blood cells can sometimes be visible on the peripheral smear.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Crisis -- History and Physical -- Aplastic Crisis
retrieved_docs.content: Aplasia in sickle crisis presents with sudden pallor and weakness confirmed by rapidly dropping hemoglobin levels accompanied by reticulocytopenia. The usual trigger for aplastic crisis is parvovirus B19, which directly suppresses the bone marrow, affecting RBC production, but other viral infections can also cause it. [12] The shortened lifespan of RBC in SCD results in the worsening of the patient's baseline anemia, which can dip to dangerously low levels. There might be a slightly low platelet count and leukopenia. The infection is self-limited, typically lasting 7 to 10 days. [9] Aplastic crises can be handled with supportive care and simple transfusions.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia (Nursing) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A complete blood count with a peripheral picture is the initial test. Here, a reduced RBC number, reduced reticulocyte count, variable mean corpuscular volume, increased leukocyte count, reduced ESR, and the presence of sickle-shaped cells in the periphery usually indicate the diagnosis of sickle cell disease. The presence of Howell-Jolly bodies indicates functional asplenia in the patient. Subsequent hemoglobin electrophoresis confirms the diagnosis of sickle cell disease if the concentration of sickle cell hemoglobin is more than 90% of the total hemoglobin and the fetal hemoglobin comprises the rest of the hemoglobin isotype. However, if the concentration of sickle cell hemoglobin is at or around 45%, it indicates the presence of the sickle cell trait rather than the disease itself. A urine analysis should be performed to rule out a urinary tract infection as a cause of hematuria and identify isosthenuria in sickle cell disease patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Evaluation -- Acute Complications in Patients with SCA
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic sequestration: Hepatic sequestration can occur across all phenotypes of SCA. Like the spleen, patients may have a baseline enlargement of the liver. Hepatic sequestration is also defined as rapid enlargement of the liver with stretching of the capsule. The hemoglobin shows a drop of more than 2gm/dL. Liver enzymes may not get elevated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2612
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is classified as a hemoglobinopathy. In the context of this disease, respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is identified as a clinical complication, and the Arabic term الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia) is likewise listed as an allowed qualifier complication. Both respiratory failure and asphyxia are closely linked to نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia), which is a direct manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية. The mapping of الاِختِنَاق to نقص الأكسجين في الدم highlights that low blood‑oxygen levels are a central pathophysiological feature that underlies these serious complications. Recognizing that sickle cell disease can precipitate severe respiratory compromise and hypoxemic states is essential for effective patient monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease. This condition is clinically associated with قَرحَة (ulcer), reflecting the propensity for vaso‑occlusive events and tissue ischemia that can lead to ulcer formation. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (blood oxygen deficiency or hypoxemia), which results from impaired red‑cell flexibility and consequent reduced oxygen delivery. Recognizing that sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) can present with ulcers and low blood‑oxygen levels is important for comprehensive patient assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with قساح. A principal manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (low blood oxygen), which is also mapped to الاِختِنَاق, indicating a link between hypoxemia and asphyxic events. Recognizing that patients with sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) may present with قساح and experience نقص الأكسجين في الدم that can progress to or be interpreted as الاِختِنَاق is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and timely therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease. داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory complications, including respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and arrest. In addition, داء الكريات المنجلية is linked to قساح, a term referring to the vaso‑occlusive crisis that characterizes acute painful episodes in this hemoglobinopathy. These interconnections underscore the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt management of crises in patients with sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2093
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hematologic disorder that can clinically manifest as adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest. This respiratory complication is classified under the broader category of respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ), which themselves are subtypes of asphyxia (الاِختِنَاق). The disease’s manifestation of low blood‑oxygen levels (نقص الأكسجين في الدم) reflects the same pathophysiologic process that defines asphyxia. Consequently, sickle cell anemia is linked to hypoxemia, which underlies the development of respiratory failure, situating the condition within a hierarchical framework that progresses from the specific hematologic disease to respiratory disorder and ultimately to the general concept of asphyxia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) is clinically associated with thalassemia (الثلاسيميا). Both disorders are hemoglobinopathies that can coexist in the same patient, and the presence of thalassemia may influence the ocular manifestations observed in sickle cell disease. Recognizing this association underscores the importance of comprehensive ophthalmic assessment and coordinated care for individuals affected by either or both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2254
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory complications, including respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest. In addition, داء الكريات المنجلية is linked to فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia). These associations highlight that patients with sickle cell disease may experience both critical respiratory events and lipid metabolism disturbances, underscoring the need for comprehensive monitoring and management of respiratory function and cholesterol levels in this population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1518
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia). The condition الاِختِنَاق (apnea) is also mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, linking apnea to the same hypoxemic state seen in sickle cell disease. Together, these relationships underscore that patients with داء الكريات المنجلية are at risk for severe respiratory complications, including hypoxemia and apnea, and highlight the importance of vigilant respiratory monitoring and oxygen management in their care.

retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as dاء الكريات المنجلية (sickle cell disease). Dاء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest, and it is also clinically associated with قساح. Radiotherapy is an allowed qualifier for both dاء الكريات المنجلية and respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult), and the use of radiotherapy can be qualified by قساح. These connections indicate that radiotherapy may be considered in the therapeutic context of sickle cell disease and adult respiratory failure, and that the presence of قساح influences how radiotherapy is qualified or applied.
